planting.note: Direct sow 2-4 weeks before last frost; successive sowings every 3 weeks extend the harvest. Autumn sowing also works well in mild climates. Plants bolt in heat, so focus on spring and fall crops.

directions: Direct sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in early spring in well-drained, moderately fertile soil; red orach is a striking annual with wine-red to burgundy foliage that makes it as ornamental as it is edible. The arrow-shaped leaves have a flavor similar to spinach — mild, slightly salty, and earthy — and are excellent raw in salads when young or lightly cooked when larger. Unlike spinach, orach is more heat-tolerant and slightly less cold-tolerant. The upright habit and vivid red color make it an excellent backdrop plant in the kitchen garden. Self-sows freely where happy, so either deadhead before seeds ripen or allow to naturalize. Young seedlings that sprout the following spring can be transplanted to preferred locations.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring or fall in sandy, saline, or well-drained soil; sea beet establishes easily from seed and can be treated as a perennial in zones 8-11 or as a cool-season annual elsewhere.

directions: Sow seeds in coastal, sandy, or well-drained soil in full sun; sea beet is the wild ancestor of all cultivated beet varieties including table beet, chard, sugar beet, and mangelwurzel — the original plant from which millennia of selection produced all these crops. It grows naturally along European and North African coastlines in dry, sandy, often saline soils and is remarkably tolerant of salt, drought, and poor soil. The glossy, dark green leaves are edible exactly like chard, with an excellent fresh flavor that is actually more tender and less strongly flavored than cultivated chard. The fleshy root is small and woody compared to cultivated beets but edible. A fascinating plant for edible garden history and for coastal gardens where most vegetables struggle.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring in moist, fertile soil; or transplant container plants in spring or fall. Once established, patience dock is virtually maintenance-free and returns reliably from the root each spring even in zone 3.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; patience dock is a large, handsome perennial with broad, spinach-like leaves that are harvested as a pot herb from earliest spring through late fall. Unlike the sharply acid sorrel (Rumex acetosa), patience dock has a mild, slightly earthy flavor with very little oxalic acid bitterness, making it more broadly useful as a cooked green. The huge leaves (up to 12 inches long) are excellent sautéed with garlic and olive oil, stirred into pasta, or used in savory pies in the way southern Europeans have used them for centuries. The plant has been cultivated in European cottage gardens for hundreds of years and was historically considered one of the most valuable early-spring pot herbs. Divide large clumps every 3-4 years to keep them productive.
sources: Cornucopia II: A Source Book of Edible Plants

planting.note: Sow in late summer or early fall in a prepared seedbed; fall-sown plants establish root systems through winter and produce more vigorous growth in spring. Spring sowing is possible but fall planting gives better first-year establishment.

directions: Sow at 8-12 lbs per acre (or approx. 1 oz per 100 sq ft) in prepared, well-drained soil in late summer; intermediate wheatgrass is a perennial cool-season grass that has been developed into the commercial grain crop Kernza by the Land Institute, representing a breakthrough in sustainable agriculture — a grain crop that doesn't need to be replanted annually, builds soil carbon, prevents erosion year-round with its deep roots, and requires far less water and fertilizer than annual wheat. The grain can be used in baking, beer, pasta, and cereals with a mild, slightly sweet flavor. Grain yield peaks in years 2-4 then gradually declines; the stand can be maintained for 5-7 years with proper management. An excellent demonstration plant for regenerative agriculture principles in larger garden plots.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds in fall for spring germination after natural cold stratification; or cold-stratify seeds in the refrigerator for 90-120 days and sow in early spring. Transplant seedlings when small, as the roots develop quickly.

directions: Plant in moist, cool, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; Rocky Mountain maple is a multi-stemmed native small tree or large shrub found from Alaska to New Mexico in canyon bottoms, streamside areas, and the cool slopes of the Rocky Mountains and Cascades. It is one of the most important browse plants for mule deer, elk, and moose in the West. The distinctive three-lobed leaves are bright green in summer, turning yellow, orange, and red in fall — a spectacular display in mountain settings. More drought-tolerant than most maples once established. The samaras are consumed by small birds and squirrels. Excellent for naturalizing in shaded, moist spots in western gardens and mountain landscapes where conditions suit its northern and montane origins.
